messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Access Control Lists (ACLs)?

Access Control Lists (ACLs): About Access Control Lists (ACLs) Access Control Lists (ACLs) let a network administrator permit or deny passage of traffic based on network addresses, protocols, service ports, and other packet attributes. ACLs are composed of one or more Access Control Entries (called ACEs). Each ACE defines a filter criteria and an action, either permit or deny. If the traffic matches the filter criteria, the specified action is taken. The permit action permits the traffic to continue through the switch. The deny action causes the traffic to be discarded (dropped). ACEs can also log or count matching traffic. Three ACL types are supported; IPv4, IPv6, and MAC. Each ACL type is focused on relevant frame or packet characteristics. ACLs must be applied (using an apply access-list command) to take effect. ACLs can be applied to interfaces (including LAGs), VLANs, or the Control Plane. Access Control Entries (ACEs) are listed according to priority by sequence number and processed in lowest to highest sequence number order.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the access-list copy command do?
The access-list copy command copies an IPv4, IPv6, or MAC ACL to a new destination ACL or overwrites an existing ACL. Syntax: access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-ACL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the access-list copy command?
Syntax: access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-ACL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the access-list log-timer command do?
The access-list log-timer command sets the log timer interval for all ACEs that have the log parameter configured. Syntax: access-list log-timer {default|<INTERVAL>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the access-list log-timer command?
Syntax: access-list log-timer {default|<INTERVAL>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the access-list mac command do?
The access-list mac command is documented in this guide. Syntax: access-list mac <ACL-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the access-list mac command?
Syntax: access-list mac <ACL-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the access-list resequence command do?
The access-list resequence command resequences the ACE sequence numbers in an ACL. Syntax: access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> resequence <STARTING-SEQUENCE-NUMBER> <INCREMENT>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the access-list resequence command?
Syntax: access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> resequence <STARTING-SEQUENCE-NUMBER> <INCREMENT>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the access-list reset command?
Syntax: access-list {all|ip <ACL-NAME>|IPv6 <ACL-NAME>|mac <ACL-NAME>} reset
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the clear access-list hitcounts control-plane command do?

The clear access-list hitcounts control-plane command clears the hit counts for ACLs applied to the Control Plane VRF. Syntax: clear access-list hitcounts [{ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME>] control-plane vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the clear access-list hitcounts control-plane command?
Syntax: clear access-list hitcounts [{ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME>] control-plane vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show access-list control-plane command do?

The show access-list control-plane command shows information about your defined ACLs that have been applied to the Control Plane. When show access-list control-plane is entered without parameters, information for all ACLs applied to the Control Plane is shown. The parameters filter the list of ACLs for which information is shown. Available filtering includes: • The content of a specific ACL that has been applied to the Control Plane. • All ACLs of a specific type that have been applied to the Control Plane. • All ACLs applied to the Control Plane for a specific VRF. Syntax: show access-list [ip|IPv6] [<ACL-NAME>] control-plane [vrf <VRF-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show access-list control-plane command?
Syntax: show access-list [ip|IPv6] [<ACL-NAME>] control-plane [vrf <VRF-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show access-list hitcounts control-plane command do?

The show access-list hitcounts control-plane command shows the hit count of the number of times an ACL (applied to the Control Plane) has matched a packet for ACEs with the count keyword. For ACEs without the count keyword, a dash is shown in place of a hit count. Syntax: show access-list hitcounts [{ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME>] control-plane vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show access-list hitcounts control-plane command?
Syntax: show access-list hitcounts [{ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME>] control-plane vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show capacities command do?
The show capacities command shows system capacities and their values for all features or a specific feature. Syntax: show capacities <FEATURE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show capacities command?
Syntax: show capacities <FEATURE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show capacities-status command do?
The show capacities-status command shows system capacities status and their values for all features or a specific feature. Syntax: show capacities-status <FEATURE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show capacities-status command?
Syntax: show capacities-status <FEATURE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Classifier policies?

Classifier policies: overview Classifier policies let a network administrator define sets of rules based on network traffic addressing or other header content, and use these rules to restrict or alter the passage of traffic through the switch. Choosing the rule criteria is called Classification, and one such rule, or list, is called a policy. Classification is achieved by creating a traffic class. The types of classes (IPv4, and IPv6) are each focused on relevant frame/ packet characteristics. Classes can be configured to match or ignore almost any frame or packet header field. Network traffic passing through a switch can be classified based on many different frame/packet characteristics including, but not limited to: •.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the apply policy command do?

The apply policy command applies a policy to the global config context. Only one policy can be globally applied at a time. Applying a policy globally again, replaces the previous globally applied policy. The no form of this command removes application of the global policy. Syntax: apply policy <POLICY-NAME> in.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the apply policy command?
Syntax: apply policy <POLICY-NAME> in
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the class copy command do?

The class copy command copies a class to a new destination class or overwrites an existing class. Copying a class copies all entries as well. Syntax: class {ip|IPv6} <CLASS-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-CLASS>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the class copy command?
Syntax: class {ip|IPv6} <CLASS-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-CLASS>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the class reset command?
Syntax: class { all | ip <CLASS-NAME> | IPv6 <CLASS-NAME>} reset
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the clear policy hitcounts command do?
The clear policy hitcounts command clears the policy hit count statistics.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the policy command do?

The policy command creates or modifies classifier policy and policy entries. A policy is made up of one or more policy entries ordered and prioritized by sequence numbers. Each entry has an IPv4/IPv6 class and zero or more policy actions associated with it. A policy must be applied using the apply command. The no form of the command can be used to delete either a policy (use no with the policy command) or an individual policy entry (use no with the sequence number). Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the policy command?
Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the policy copy command do?

The policy copy command copies a policy to a new destination policy or overwrites an existing policy. Copying a policy copies all its entries as well. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-POLICY>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the policy copy command?
Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-POLICY>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the policy resequence command do?
The policy resequence command resequences numbering in a policy. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> resequence <STARTING-SEQ-NUM> <INCREMENT>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the policy resequence command?
Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> resequence <STARTING-SEQ-NUM> <INCREMENT>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the policy reset command do?

The policy reset command changes the user-specified policy configuration to match the active policy configuration. Use this command when a discrepancy exists between what the user configured and what is active and accepted by the system. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> reset.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the policy reset command?
Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> reset

section: ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what restrictions or notes are documented for ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations?

For ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: TCAM lookups There are four TCAM lookups available to use for these features. Each of these features uses one TCAM lookup when enabled. At most, four of these features can be enabled at the same time. Ingress Port IP ACL Ingress Port MAC ACL Ingress Port Policy Ingress Port Access Client Policy Ingress VLAN IP ACL Ingress VLAN MAC ACL Ingress VLAN Policy Ingress Global Policy.

topic: ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Port Access Client Policy?

Port Access Client Policy: Matching precedence order When a packet is matched by multiple TCAM Lookups with the same action, a precedence order is followed. The precedence order from highest to lowest is as follows: Meter Actions: Port Access Client Policy Port Policy VLAN Policy.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Ingress Global Policy?

Ingress Global Policy: QoS Actions: Port Access Client Policy Remark Ingress Port Policy Remark Ingress VLAN Policy Remark Ingress Global Policy Remark QoS DSCP Map Entry QoS COS Map Entry QoS Port Config MAC Port ACL Logging IP Port ACL Logging MAC VLAN ACL Logging.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show resources command do?

The show resources command shows hardware resource consumption. Resource data is updated every 10 seconds. Hardware resource consumption information is shown for: • TCAM entries • TCAM lookups • Policers. Syntax: show resources.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show resources command?
Syntax: show resources
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the access-list mac <ACL-NAME> command do?
The access-list mac <ACL-NAME> command is documented in this guide. Syntax: no access-list mac <ACL-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the access-list mac <ACL-NAME> command?
Syntax: no access-list mac <ACL-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> command do?

The IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> command specifies a permanent static neighbor entry in the ARP table (for IPv6 neighbors). The no form of this command deletes a permanent static neighbor entry from the ARP table. Syntax: no IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> command?
Syntax: no IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the checkpoint post-configuration command do?

The checkpoint post-configuration command enables creation of system generated checkpoints when configuration changes occur. This feature is enabled by default. The no form of this command disables system generated checkpoints. Syntax: no checkpoint post-configuration.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint post-configuration command?
Syntax: no checkpoint post-configuration
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} command do?
The apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} command is documented in this guide. Syntax: - no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} - apply policy <POLICY-NAME> in - no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> in.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} command?
Syntax: - no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} - apply policy <POLICY-NAME> in - no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> in
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command allows all packets related to the CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) on a secure port. The no form of this command blocks the CDP BPDU on a secure port. On a nonsecure port, the command has no effect. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command?
Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip dns domain-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [vrf default] command do?

The ip dns domain-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [vrf default] command configures one or more domain names that are appended to the DNS request. The DNS client appends each name in succession until the DNS server replies. Domains can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. The no form of this command removes a domain from the list. Syntax: no ip dns domain-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [vrf default].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip dns domain-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [vrf default] command?
Syntax: no ip dns domain-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [vrf default]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command do?

The ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. The no form of this command removes the domain name. Syntax: no ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command?
Syntax: no ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip dns host <HOST-NAME> <IP-ADDR> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command do?

The ip dns host <HOST-NAME> <IP-ADDR> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. Syntax: no ip dns host <HOST-NAME> <IP-ADDR> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip dns host <HOST-NAME> <IP-ADDR> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command?
Syntax: no ip dns host <HOST-NAME> <IP-ADDR> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the erps ring <ringid> command do?

The erps ring <ringid> command this command creates an ERPS ring with a given ID. The no form of this command removes all the configurations of the ring, including instances. This command configures the ERPS ring member port. An L2 interface in the switch is associated to one of the two member ports of an ERPS ring. In case of an interconnection node, only port0 is applicable for the sub-ring. The no form of this command removes the association of the ring port to the L2 interface on the switch. This command adds descriptive information to help administrators and operators understand the purpose of a ring. 1-64 printable ASCII characters are allowed. The no form of this command removes the ring instance description. Syntax: erps ring <ringid>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the erps ring <ringid> command?
Syntax: erps ring <ringid>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the apply fault-monitor profile <profile-name> command do?

The apply fault-monitor profile <profile-name> command configures the fault monitoring profile to an interface. The profile is allowed to be modified after it is applied on a port. The no form of this command removes the fault monitoring profile from an interface. Syntax: no apply fault-monitor profile <profile-name>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the apply fault-monitor profile <profile-name> command?
Syntax: no apply fault-monitor profile <profile-name>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vsx-sync command do?
The vsx-sync command configures VSX synchronization for a fault monitoring profile. The no form of this command removes the VSX synchronization for a fault monitoring profile. Syntax: no vsx-sync.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the vsx-sync command?
Syntax: no vsx-sync
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. NOTE: This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Syntax: no ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command configures an IGMP last member query interval value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the value to a default of 1 second on an interface. Chapter 33 IGMP commands 353. Syntax: no ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp querier command do?

The ip igmp querier command configures an IGMP querier on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. This functionality will allow an interface to join in the querier-election process. The no form of this command disables IGMP querier on an interface. Configures the interval between IGMP queries on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the IGMP querier interval to the default value of 125 seconds on an interface. Syntax: ip igmp querier interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp querier command?
Syntax: ip igmp querier interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME> command do?

The ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME> command configures the IGMP querier max response time value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the querier max response time value to the default of 10 seconds on an interface. Syntax: no ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command configures IGMP robustness on an interface, depending on the command context. The robustness parameter allows tuning for the expected packet loss on a subnet. The no form of this command sets the robustness value to the default of 2 on an interface. Syntax: no ip igmp robustness <VALUE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp robustness <VALUE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. 372 AOS-CX 10.06 Command-Line Interface Guide NOTE: This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

syntax: no ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

syntax: no ip igmp snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp snooping auto <PORT-LIST> command do?

The ip igmp snooping auto <PORT-LIST> command configures the specified ports in auto mode. In auto mode traffic flow is controlled by the IGMP joins/leaves. Auto mode is the default. The no form of this command removes auto mode ports for the VLAN. Chapter 34 IGMP Snooping commands 373. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping auto <PORT-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp snooping auto <PORT-LIST> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp snooping auto <PORT-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp snooping fastlearn <PORT-LIST> command do?

The ip igmp snooping fastlearn <PORT-LIST> command enables the port to learn group information when receiving a topology change notification. By default, fast learn is not enabled on ports. The no form of this command disables fast learn on the specified ports. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping fastlearn <PORT-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp snooping fastlearn <PORT-LIST> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp snooping fastlearn <PORT-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp snooping fastleave <PORT-LIST> command do?

The ip igmp snooping fastleave <PORT-LIST> command enables the switch to immediately remove the IGMP client from its IGMP table and cease transmitting multicast traffic to the client. The no form of this command disables fastleave on the specified ports. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping fastleave <PORT-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp snooping fastleave <PORT-LIST> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp snooping fastleave <PORT-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp snooping forced-fastleave <PORT-LIST> command do?

The ip igmp snooping forced-fastleave <PORT-LIST> command configures the specified ports in forced fastleave mode. The no form of this command disables forced fastleave on the specified ports. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping forced-fastleave <PORT-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp snooping forced-fastleave <PORT-LIST> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp snooping forced-fastleave <PORT-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip igmp snooping forward <PORT-LIST> command do?

The ip igmp snooping forward <PORT-LIST> command configures the specified ports in forward mode. In forward mode, traffic is always forwarded on this port, irrespective of joins. The no form of this command disables forward ports. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping forward <PORT-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip igmp snooping forward <PORT-LIST> command?
Syntax: no ip igmp snooping forward <PORT-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command do?

The interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command creates an interface VLAN also know as an SVI (switched virtual interface) and changes to the config-if- vlan context. The specified VLAN must already be defined on the switch. The no form of this command deletes an interface VLAN. Syntax: no interface vlan <VLAN-ID>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command?
Syntax: no interface vlan <VLAN-ID>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the interface tunnel <EXISTING-TUNNEL-NUMBER> command do?

The interface tunnel <EXISTING-TUNNEL-NUMBER> command creates or updates an IP tunnel. After you enter the command, the firmware switches to the configuration context for the tunnel. If the specified tunnel exists, this command switches to the context for the tunnel. By default, all tunnels are automatically assigned to the default VRF when they are created. The no form of this command deletes an existing IP tunnel. Syntax: no interface tunnel <EXISTING-TUNNEL-NUMBER>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the interface tunnel <EXISTING-TUNNEL-NUMBER> command?
Syntax: no interface tunnel <EXISTING-TUNNEL-NUMBER>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command?
Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME> command do?

The IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME> command adds static IPv6 client source binding information to the switch IPv6 binding database. Although DHCPv6 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IPv6 binding database. NOTE: Statically configured IPv6 binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv6 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv6 snooping. Syntax: no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME> command?
Syntax: no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 source-lockdown command do?

The IPv6 source-lockdown command enables IPv6 source lockdown for all VLANs on the selected interface (port). The no form of this command disables IPv6 source lockdown for the selected interface (port). Retries the IPv6 source lockdown hardware programming for a client identified by VLAN and IPv6 address. Shows all IPv6 static source binding information irrespective of source lockdown configuration. Shows summary or detailed IPv6 source lockdown information. When entered without parameters, summary status information for all interfaces (ports) in the binding database is shown. Syntax: IPv6 source-lockdown hardware retry <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 source-lockdown command?
Syntax: IPv6 source-lockdown hardware retry <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command do?

The IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command sets a global unicast address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the global unicast address on the interface. NOTE: This command automatically creates an IPv6 link-local address on the interface. However, it does not add the command to the running configuration. If you remove the IPv6 address, the link-local address is also removed. To maintain the link-local address, you must manually execute the command. Syntax: no IPv6 address <global-unicast-address>.

Syntax: no IPv6 address <global-unicast-address>

The ssh password-authentication command enables the password-based authentication method for use with SSH clients. The no form of this command disables the password-based authentication method for use with SSH clients. Syntax: no ssh password-authentication.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh password-authentication command?
Syntax: no ssh password-authentication
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the interface loopback <INSTANCE> command do?

The interface loopback <INSTANCE> command creates a loopback interface and enters loopback configuration mode. The no form of this command deletes a loopback interface. Syntax: no interface loopback <INSTANCE>.

For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no ntp server <IP-ADDR> command do?

The no ntp server <IP-ADDR> command defines an NTP server to use for time synchronization, or updates the settings of an existing server with new values. Up to eight servers can be defined. The no form of this command removes a configured NTP server. Syntax: no ntp server <IP-ADDR> [burst] [iburst] [prefer] [key-id <KEY-NUM>].

For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip ospf <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The ip ospf <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPF protocol on the interface with the configured IPv4 address for the area specified. The interfaces which have an IP address configured in this network or in a subset of this network, will participate in the OSPF protocol. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol. Sets the authentication type that will be used for authentication with the neighbor router. Syntax: ip ospf authentication {message-digest | simple-text | null | keychain}.

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip ospf <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command?
Syntax: ip ospf authentication {message-digest | simple-text | null | keychain}

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol Configures null authentication on an interface which disables IPsec authentication. Configures IPSec AH authentication. OSPFv3 interfaces that have IPsec configured at the interface context will not use area level IPsec. The no form of this command removes IPsec AH authentication for the specified area. Sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command?
Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command creates a leaf certificate and changes to its context config-cert-<CERT-NAME>. If the specified leaf certificate exists, this command changes to its context. The first time the switch starts it creates a self-signed, default leaf certificate called local-cert. This certificate is used by any switch application that does not have an associated leaf certificate. The no form of this command deletes the specified leaf certificate. The default leaf certificate local-cert cannot be deleted. Syntax: no crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>.

Syntax: no crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method eap-radius command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method eap-radius command configures the authentication mechanism used to control access to the network. The configured authentication method will be used to authenticate 802.1X clients. The no form of the command resets the authentication mechanism to the default, eap-radius. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method eap-radius.

section: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth command enables cached reauthentication on a port. Cached reauthentication allows 802.1X reauthentications to succeed when the RADIUS server is unavailable. Users already authenticated retain their currently assigned RADIUS attributes. The no form of the command disables the cached reauthentication on a port. Configures the period during which an authenticated client, which has failed to reauthenticate because the RADIUS server is unreachable, remains authenticated. The no form of the command resets the cached reauthentication period to the default, 30 seconds. Configures the period the port waits to retransmit the next EAPOL request identity frame on an 802.1X enabled port that has no authenticated clients. The no form of the command resets the discovery period to the default, 30 seconds. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>.

command: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth
syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>

command: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth

section: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator reauth

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator reauth command enables periodic reauthentication of authenticated clients on the port. The no form of the command disables periodic reauthentication. Configures the period after which the authenticated clients are reauthenticated on the port. You must enable reauthentication on the port before configuring the reauthentication period. The no form of the command resets the reauthentication period to the default, 3600 seconds. Clears the 802.1X authentication statistics associated with the port and all the authenticator clients attached to this port. If no interface is specified, the statistics is cleared for all 802.1X enabled ports. Shows information about active 802.1X authentication sessions. The output can be filtered by interface or MAC address. Shows information about 802.1X ports. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator reauth-period <PERIOD>.

command: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator reauth
syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator reauth-period <PERIOD>

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command enables cached reauthentication on a port. Cached reauthentication allows MAC reauthentications to succeed when the RADIUS server is unavailable. Users who are already authenticated, retain their currently assigned RADIUS attributes. The no form of the command disables cached reauthentication. Configures the period during which an authenticated client, which has failed to reauthenticate because the RADIUS server is unreachable, remains authenticated. The no form of the command resets the cached reauthentication period to the default, 30 seconds. Enables and configures the global password that the switch must use for MAC authentication. The password can be either in ciphertext or plaintext format. The no form of the command disables the password for MAC authentication. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth command enables periodic MAC reauthentication of authenticated clients on the port. The no form of the command disables periodic MAC reauthentication on the port. Configures the period after which MAC authenticated clients must be reauthenticated on the port. You must first enable MAC reauthentication on the port before configuring the MAC reauthentication period. The no form of the command resets the MAC reauthentication period to the default, 3600 seconds. Clears the MAC authentication statistics associated with the port and all the authenticator state machines associated to this port. If no interface is specified, the statistics is cleared for all MAC authentication enabled ports. Shows information about MAC authentication clients status. The output can be filtered by interface or MAC address. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth-period <PERIOD>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth-period <PERIOD>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the qos queue-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos queue-profile <NAME> command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. A queue profile named factory-default is defined by default and is automatically applied to all interfaces. It cannot be edited or deleted. Use the command show qos queue-profile factory- default to view this profile. Do not use show running-configuration, as it will only display changes from the initial values. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Syntax: no qos queue-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vlan <VLAN-LIST> command do?

The vlan <VLAN-LIST> command creates a VLAN and changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. By default, the VLAN is enabled. To disable a VLAN, use the no shutdown command. If the specified VLAN exists, this command changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. If a range of VLANs is specified, the context does not change. The no form of this command removes a VLAN. VLAN 1 is the default VLAN and cannot be deleted. Creates an access interface and assigns an VLAN ID to it. Only one VLAN ID can be assigned to each access interface. VLANs can only be assigned to a non-routed (layer 2) interface or LAG interface. By default, all interfaces are routed (layer 3) when created. Use the no routing command to disable routing on an interface and change the interface to a layer 2 interface. Syntax: vlan access <VLAN-ID>.

For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Management of rotated log files?

Management of rotated log files: Rotated log files are compressed and stored locally in /var/log/, regardless of the remote host configuration. Rotated log files are stored with respective time extension to the granularity of hour in the format file1-YYYYMMDDHH.gz (for example, messages-2015080715.gz). Rotated log files are replaced when the number of old rotated log files exceeds three. The newly rotated log file replaces the oldest rotated log file. Remote transfer of rotated log files Only the TFTP protocol is supported for remote transfer, and both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are supported. Only newly rotated log files are transferred to the remote host during the log rotation. Previously rotated log files are not transferred. After a file is successfully transferred, it is removed from the switch local path.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE> command do?

The logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE> command specifies the maximum allowed log file size. The no form of this command resets the size of the log file to the default (100 MB). Sets the rotate period for the event logs, stored in the /var/log/event.log file, and authentication logs, stored in the /var/log/auth.log file. Defaults to daily. A log file that exceeds either the logrotate <MAX-SIZE> value or the logrotate period (whichever happens first), triggers rotation for that log file. The no form of this command resets the log rotation period to the default. Syntax: logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE>.

Syntax: logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE>

For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the system resource-utilization poll-interval <SECONDS> command do?

The system resource-utilization poll-interval <SECONDS> command shows version information about the network operating system software, service operating system software, and BIOS. Configures the polling interval for system resource information collection and recording such as CPU and memory usage. Shows CPU utilization information. Shows memory utilization information. Syntax: system resource-utilization poll-interval <SECONDS>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the system resource-utilization poll-interval <SECONDS> command?
Syntax: system resource-utilization poll-interval <SECONDS>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the usb command do?

The usb command enables the USB ports on the switch. This setting is persistent across switch reboots and management module failovers. Both active and standby management modules are affected by this setting. The no form of this command disables the USB ports. Enables or disables a USB drive in a USB port. Syntax: usb.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the usb command?
Syntax: usb
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the copy command-output command do?
The copy command-output command copies the specified command output using TFTP, SFTP, or USB. Syntax: copy command-output "<COMMAND>" {<STORAGE-URL> | <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy command-output command?
Syntax: copy command-output "<COMMAND>" {<STORAGE-URL> | <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the copy diag-dump feature <FEATURE> command do?

The copy diag-dump feature <FEATURE> command copies the specified diagnostic information using TFTP, SFTP, or USB. Syntax: copy diag-dump feature <FEATURE> {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy diag-dump feature <FEATURE> command?
Syntax: copy diag-dump feature <FEATURE> {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the copy diag-dump local-file command do?

The copy diag-dump local-file command copies the diagnostic information stored in a local file using TFTP, SFTP, or USB. Syntax: copy diag-dump local-file {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy diag-dump local-file command?
Syntax: copy diag-dump local-file {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the copy show-tech feature command do?
The copy show-tech feature command copies show tech output using TFTP, SFTP, and USB. Syntax: copy show-tech feature <FEATURE> {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy show-tech feature command?
Syntax: copy show-tech feature <FEATURE> {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the copy show-tech local-file command do?
The copy show-tech local-file command copies show tech output stored in a local file. Syntax: copy show-tech local-file {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy show-tech local-file command?
Syntax: copy show-tech local-file {<REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] | <STORAGE-URL>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the traceroute command do?

The traceroute command uses traceroute for the specified IPv4 address or hostname with or without optional parameters. Syntax: traceroute {IPv4-address | hostname} [ip-option loosesourceroute <IPv4-ADDR>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the traceroute command?
Syntax: traceroute {IPv4-address | hostname} [ip-option loosesourceroute <IPv4-ADDR>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you troubleshoot?

To troubleshoot, follow the documented procedure: 1. Modify the ACL to allow the ping traffic 2. Unapply the ACL from egress (8400/8320/8325 switches) or Control Plane 3. Ping a destination which is not matched by the ACL. For example, if the ACL is blocking traffic based on. Commands shown in the procedure include: PING 100.1.2.10 (100.1.2.10) 100(128) bytes of data; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the logging command do?

The logging command enables syslog forwarding to a remote syslog server. The no form of this command disables syslog forwarding to a remote syslog server. Syntax: logging {<IPv4-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR> | <HOSTNAME>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the logging command?
Syntax: logging {<IPv4-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR> | <HOSTNAME>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the logging persistent-storage command do?

The logging persistent-storage command enables or disables storage of logs in storage. Only logs of the specified severity and above will be preserved in the storage. Syntax: logging persistent-storage [severity {alert|crit|debug|emerg|err|info|notice|warning}].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the logging persistent-storage command?
Syntax: logging persistent-storage [severity {alert|crit|debug|emerg|err|info|notice|warning}]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the diag on-demand command do?
The diag on-demand command runs the diagnostic tests for all modules or for a specified module. Syntax: diag on-demand {line-module | management-module} [<SLOT-ID>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the diag on-demand command?
Syntax: diag on-demand {line-module | management-module} [<SLOT-ID>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show diagnostic events command do?
The show diagnostic events command displays the diagnostic related event logs. Syntax: show diagnostic events.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show diagnostic events command?
Syntax: show diagnostic events
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the boot command do?
The boot command presents you with the boot menu prompt. You can then specify which boot profile: primary, secondary, or Service OS console. Syntax: boot.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the boot command?
Syntax: boot
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the erase zeroize command do?

The erase zeroize command securely erases any user data contained on the eMMC or other storage devices on the management module. NOTE: Back up all data before running this command or all user/config data will be lost. Syntax: erase zeroize.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the erase zeroize command?
Syntax: erase zeroize
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the md5sum [-c | -s | -w] [<FILE-NAME>] command do?
The md5sum [-c | -s | -w] [<FILE-NAME>] command this command computes and checks the MD5 message digest. Syntax: md5sum [-c | -s | -w] [<FILE-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the md5sum [-c | -s | -w] [<FILE-NAME>] command?
Syntax: md5sum [-c | -s | -w] [<FILE-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the mkdir [-m | -p] [<DIRECTORY-NAME>] command do?

The mkdir [-m | -p] [<DIRECTORY-NAME>] command this command makes directories. This command mounts the eMMC partitions to the following locations: /coredump, /logs, /nos, / selftest, and mounts the USB device to /mnt/usb. Users can mount USB flash drives formatted as either FAT16 or FAT32 with a single partition. This command moves (renames) files. Syntax: mkdir [-m | -p] [<DIRECTORY-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the mkdir [-m | -p] [<DIRECTORY-NAME>] command?
Syntax: mkdir [-m | -p] [<DIRECTORY-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the pwd command do?
The pwd command displays the current working directory. Syntax: pwd.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the pwd command?
Syntax: pwd
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the reboot command do?
The reboot command reboots the Management Module. Syntax: reboot.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the reboot command?
Syntax: reboot
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the rm command do?
The rm command removes files or directories. Syntax: rm [-f | -i | -R | -r] <FILE-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the rm command?
Syntax: rm [-f | -i | -R | -r] <FILE-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the rmdir command do?
The rmdir command removes empty directories. Syntax: rmdir [-p] <DIRECTORY-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the rmdir command?
Syntax: rmdir [-p] <DIRECTORY-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the secure-mode command do?

The secure-mode command sets the secure mode to enhanced or standard secure mode. Also can display the current secure mode. A zeroization is required before switching between enhanced and standard secure modes. The command also displays a message notifying the user that they are already in the targeted secure mode. Syntax: secure-mode <enhanced | standard | status>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the secure-mode command?
Syntax: secure-mode <enhanced | standard | status>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the sh command do?

The sh command launches a bash shell for support purposes. To quit bash, enter exit. This command is not available if enhanced secure mode is set. Unmounts the eMMC partitions mounted to the following locations: /coredump, /logs, /nos, /selftest, and unmounts the USB device mounted to /mnt/usb. Verifies and installs a product image. The user can select the primary or secondary boot profile to update and the location of the file. Syntax: sh.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the sh command?
Syntax: sh
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the version command do?
The version command displays the following build strings: • Version. • Build date. • Build time. • Build ID. • SHA. Syntax: version.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the version command?
Syntax: version
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is In-System Programming?

In-System Programming: The ISP (In-System Programming) feature provides an automated way to roll out updates to various programmable devices in an AOS-CX network switch, after the product has shipped. ISP is intended to run automatically either at boot time or as new modules are inserted into the chassis at runtime. Show tech command list for the ISP feature Task Displaying versions of all present programmable devices. Displaying stored log files from any ISP updates on the system.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the clear update-log command do?
The clear update-log command clears stored log files of any In-System Programming updates on the system. Syntax: clear update-log.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the clear update-log command?
Syntax: clear update-log
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show needed-updates command do?
The show needed-updates command displays whether any programmable devices are in need of an update. Syntax: show needed-updates [next-boot [primary|secondary]].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show needed-updates command?
Syntax: show needed-updates [next-boot [primary|secondary]]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the fastboot command do?
The fastboot command enables fastboot for the system. The no form of this command disables fastboot for the system. Syntax: fastboot.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the fastboot command?
Syntax: fastboot
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show selftest command do?
The show selftest command displays selftest results. Syntax: show selftest [brief].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show selftest command?
Syntax: show selftest [brief]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the erase all zeroize command do?

The erase all zeroize command restores the switch to its factory default configuration. You will be prompted before the procedure starts. Once complete, the switch will restart from the primary image with factory default settings. NOTE: Back up all data before running this command as all configuration settings will be lost. Syntax: erase all zeroize.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the erase all zeroize command?
Syntax: erase all zeroize
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the terminal-monitor {notify <event|debug|all> | severity <level> | filter keyword} command?
Syntax: terminal-monitor {notify <event|debug|all> | severity <level> | filter keyword}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show terminal-monitor command do?
The show terminal-monitor command shows whether the terminal monitoring is enabled or disabled. Syntax: show terminal-monitor.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show terminal-monitor command?
Syntax: show terminal-monitor

section: Troubleshooting Web UI and REST API access issues

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you troubleshoot Web UI and REST API access issues?

To troubleshoot Web UI and REST API access issues, follow the documented procedure: 1. Log out from one of the existing sessions 2. If the session cookie is lost and it is not possible to log out of the session, then wait for the session idle.

task: Troubleshooting Web UI and REST API access issues

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about About AOS-CX?

About AOS-CX: AOS-CX introduction AOS-CX is a new, modern, fully programmable operating system built using a database-centric design that ensures higher availability and dynamic software process changes for reduced downtime. In addition to robust hardware reliability, the AOS-CX operating system includes additional software elements not available with traditional systems, including: • Automated visibility to help IT organizations scale: The Aruba Network Analytics Engine allows IT to monitor and troubleshoot network, system, application, and security-related issues easily through simple scripts. This engine comes with a built-in time series database that enables customers and developers to create software modules that allow historical troubleshooting, as well as analysis of historical trends to predict and avoid future problems due to scale, security, and performance bottlenecks.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Initial configuration?

Initial configuration: Perform the initial configuration of a factory default switch using one of the following methods: • Load a switch configuration using zero-touch provisioning (ZTP). When ZTP is used, the configuration is loaded from a server automatically when the switch booted from the factory default configuration. • Connect the management port on the switch to your network, and then use SSH client software to reach the switch from a computer connected to the same network. This requires that a DHCP server is installed on the network. Configure switch settings and features by executing CLI commands. • Connect a computer running terminal emulation software to the console port on the switch. Configure switch settings and features by executing CLI commands. Initial configuration using ZTP Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP) configures a switch automatically from a remote server. Prerequisites • The switch must be in the factory default configuration. Do not change the configuration of the switch from its factory default configuration in any way, including by setting the administrator password.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you configure banners?

To configure banners, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure the banner that is displayed when a user connects to a device using a console port or in-band 2. Configure the banner that is displayed after a user is authenticated. Use the command banner exec 3. Log in to the CLI 4. Switch to config context.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you configure the in-band management interface?

To configure the in-band management interface, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to the in-band management interface context with the command interface vlan 1 2. SSH is enabled by default on the default VRF. If disabled, enable SSH with the command ssh server. Commands shown in the procedure include: vrf default..
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp authentication command do?
The ntp authentication command enables support for authentication when communicating with an NTP server. The no form of this command disables authentication support. Syntax: ntp authentication.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp authentication command?
Syntax: ntp authentication
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp authentication-key command do?

The ntp authentication-key command defines an authentication key that is used to secure the exchange with an NTP time server. This command provides protection against accidentally synchronizing to a time source that is not trusted. The no form of this command removes an authentication key. Syntax: ntp authentication-key <KEY-ID> {md5 | sha1} <PASSWORD> [trusted].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp authentication-key command?
Syntax: ntp authentication-key <KEY-ID> {md5 | sha1} <PASSWORD> [trusted]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp disable command do?
The ntp disable command is documented in this guide. Syntax: ntp disable.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp disable command?
Syntax: ntp disable
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp enable command do?

The ntp enable command enables the NTP client on the switch to automatically adjust the local time and date on the switch. The NTP client is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables the NTP client. Syntax: ntp enable.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp enable command?
Syntax: ntp enable
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp server command do?

The ntp server command defines an NTP server to use for time synchronization, or updates the settings of an existing server with new values. Up to eight servers can be defined. The no form of this command removes a configured NTP server. Syntax: ntp server <IP-ADDR> [key <KEY-NUM>] [minpoll <MIN-NUM>] [maxpoll <MAX-NUM>][burst | iburst] [prefer] [version <VER-NUM>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp server command?
Syntax: ntp server <IP-ADDR> [key <KEY-NUM>] [minpoll <MIN-NUM>] [maxpoll <MAX-NUM>][burst | iburst] [prefer] [version <VER-NUM>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp trusted-key command do?

The ntp trusted-key command sets a key as trusted. When NTP authentication is enabled, the switch only synchronizes with time servers that transmit packets containing a trusted key. The no form of this command removes the trusted designation from a key. Syntax: ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp trusted-key command?
Syntax: ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ntp vrf command do?
The ntp vrf command nOTE: 6100 only supports default VRF. Specifies the VRF on which the NTP client communicates with an NTP server. 28 AOS-CX 10.06 Fundamentals Guide. Syntax: ntp vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ntp vrf command?
Syntax: ntp vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ntp associations command do?

The show ntp associations command shows the status of the connection to each NTP server. The following information is displayed for each server: • Tally code: The first character is the Tally code: ◦ ◦ ◦ ◦ (blank): No state information available (e.g. non-responding server) x: Out of tolerance (discarded by intersection algorithm).: Discarded by table overflow (not used) -: Out of tolerance (discarded by the cluster algorithm) ◦ +: Good and a preferred remote peer or server (included by the combine algorithm) ◦ #: Good remote peer or server, but not utilized (ready as a backup source) ◦ *: Remote peer or server presently used as a primary reference ◦ o: PPS peer (when the prefer peer is valid) • ID: Server number. • NAME: NTP server FQDN/IP address (Only the first 24 characters of the name are displayed). Syntax: show ntp associations.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ntp associations command?
Syntax: show ntp associations
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ntp authentication-keys command do?
The show ntp authentication-keys command shows the currently defined authentication keys. Syntax: show ntp authentication-keys.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ntp authentication-keys command?
Syntax: show ntp authentication-keys
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ntp servers command?
Syntax: show ntp servers
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ntp statistics command do?

The show ntp statistics command shows global NTP statistics. The following information is displayed: • Rx-pkts: Total NTP packets received. • Current Version Rx-pkts: Number of NTP packets that match the current NTP version. • Old Version Rx-pkts: Number of NTP packets that match the previous NTP version. • Error pkts: Packets dropped due to all other error reasons. • Auth-failed pkts: Packets dropped due to authentication failure. • Declined pkts: Packets denied access for any reason. • Restricted pkts: Packets dropped due to NTP access control. • Rate-limited pkts: Number of packets discarded due to rate limitation. • KOD pkts: Number of Kiss of Death packets sent. Syntax: show ntp statistics.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ntp statistics command?
Syntax: show ntp statistics
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ntp status command do?
The show ntp status command shows the status of NTP on the switch. Syntax: show ntp status.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ntp status command?
Syntax: show ntp status
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you configure a layer 2 interface?

To configure a layer 2 interface, follow the documented procedure: 1. Change to the interface configuration context for the interface with the command interface 2. Set the interface MTU (maximum transmission unit) with the command mtu 3. Review interface configuration settings with the command show interface.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the interface command do?

The interface command switches to the config-if context for a physical port. This is where you define the configuration settings for the logical interface associated with the physical port. Syntax: interface <PORT-NUM>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the interface command?
Syntax: interface <PORT-NUM>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command creates an interface VLAN also know as an SVI (switched virtual interface) and changes to the config-if- vlan context. The specified VLAN must already be defined on the switch. The no form of this command deletes an interface VLAN.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip address command do?
The ip address command sets an IP/IPv6 address on the interface VLAN. The no form of this command removes the IP/IPv6 address from the interface. Syntax: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip address command?
Syntax: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip mtu command do?

The ip mtu command sets the IP MTU (maximum transmission unit) for an interface. This defines the largest IP packet that can be sent or received by the interface. The no form of this command sets the IP MTU to the default value 1500. Syntax: ip mtu <VALUE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip mtu command?
Syntax: ip mtu <VALUE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address command?
Syntax: IPv6 address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>{eui64 | [tag <ID>]}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show allow-unsupported-transceiver command do?
The show allow-unsupported-transceiver command displays configuration and status of unsupported transceivers. Syntax: show allow-unsupported-transceiver.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show allow-unsupported-transceiver command?
Syntax: show allow-unsupported-transceiver
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show interface command do?
The show interface command displays active configurations and operational status information for interfaces. Syntax: show interface [<IFNNAME>|<IFRANGE>] [brief | physical | extended [non-zero]].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show interface command?
Syntax: show interface [<IFNNAME>|<IFRANGE>] [brief | physical | extended [non-zero]]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show interface dom command do?

The show interface dom command shows diagnostics information and alarm/warning flags for the optical transceivers (SFP, SFP+). This information is known as DOM (Digital Optical Monitoring). DOM information also consists of vendor determined thresholds which trigger high/low alarms and warning flags. Syntax: show interface [<INTERFACE-ID>] dom [detail].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show interface dom command?
Syntax: show interface [<INTERFACE-ID>] dom [detail]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show interface transceiver command do?
The show interface transceiver command is documented in this guide. Syntax: show interface [<INTERFACE-ID>] transceiver [detail | threshold-violations].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show interface transceiver command?
Syntax: show interface [<INTERFACE-ID>] transceiver [detail | threshold-violations]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ip interface command do?
The show ip interface command shows status and configuration information for an IPv4 interface. Syntax: show ip interface <INTERFACE-ID>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ip interface command?
Syntax: show ip interface <INTERFACE-ID>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ip source-interface command?
Syntax: show ip source-interface {sflow | tftp | radius | tacacs | all} [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

syntax: show ip source-interface {sflow | tftp | radius | tacacs | all} [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show IPv6 interface command do?
The show IPv6 interface command shows status and configuration information for an IPv6 interface. Syntax: show IPv6 interface <INTERFACE-ID>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show IPv6 interface command?
Syntax: show IPv6 interface <INTERFACE-ID>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show IPv6 source-interface command?
Syntax: show IPv6 source-interface {sflow | tftp | radius | tacacs | all} [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

syntax: show IPv6 source-interface {sflow | tftp | radius | tacacs | all} [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the shutdown command do?
The shutdown command disables an interface. Interfaces are disabled by default when created. The no form of this command enables an interface. Syntax: shutdown.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the shutdown command?
Syntax: shutdown
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VLANs?

VLANs: are primarily used to provide network segmentation at layer 2. VLANs enable the grouping of users by logical function instead of physical location. They make managing bandwidth usage within networks possible by: • Allowing grouping of high-bandwidth users on low-traffic segments • Organizing users from different LAN segments according to their need for common resources and individual protocols • Improving traffic control at the edge of networks by separating traffic of different protocol types. • Enhancing network security by creating subnets to control in-band access to specific network resources VLANs are generally assigned on an organizational basis rather than on a physical basis. For example, a network administrator could assign all workstations and servers used by a particular workgroup to the same VLAN, regardless of their physical locations. Hosts in the same VLAN can directly communicate with one another. A router or a Layer 3 switch is required for hosts in different VLANs to communicate with one another.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you create and enabling a VLAN?
To create and enabling a VLAN, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to the configuration context with the command config 2. Create a new VLAN with the command vlan.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you disable a VLAN?

To disable a VLAN, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to configuration context with the command config 2. Switch to configuration context for the VLAN you want to disable with the command vlan 3. Disable the VLAN with the command shutdown.

section: Assigning a VLAN to an interface

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Assigning a VLAN to an interface?

Assigning a VLAN to an interface: To use a VLAN, it must be assigned to an interface on the switch. VLANs can only be assigned to non-routed (layer 2) interfaces. All interfaces are routed (layer 3) by default when created. Use the no routing command to disable routing on an interface.

topic: Assigning a VLAN to an interface

section: Assigning a VLAN ID to an access interface

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Assigning a VLAN ID to an access interface?

Assigning a VLAN ID to an access interface: Prerequisites At least one defined VLAN. Procedure 1. Switch to configuration context with the command config. 2. Switch to the interface that you want to define as an access interface with the command interface. 3. Configure the access interface and assign a VLAN ID with the command vlan access. Examples.

topic: Assigning a VLAN ID to an access interface

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show capacities svi-count command do?
The show capacities svi-count command shows the maximum number of SVIs supported by the switch. Syntax: show capacities svi-count.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show capacities svi-count command?
Syntax: show capacities svi-count
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vlan [<VLAN-ID>] command do?
The show vlan [<VLAN-ID>] command displays configuration information for all VLANs or a specific VLAN.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vlan port command?
Syntax: show vlan port <INTERFACE-ID>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show VLANsummary command do?
The show VLAN summary command displays a summary of the VLAN configuration on the switch. Syntax: show vlan summary.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show VLANsummary command?
Syntax: show vlan summary
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no vlan <VLAN-LIST> command do?

The no vlan <VLAN-LIST> command creates a VLAN and changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. By default, the VLAN is enabled. To disable a VLAN, use the no shutdown command. If the specified VLAN exists, this command changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. If a range of VLANs is specified, the context does not change. The no form of this command removes a VLAN. VLAN 1 is the default VLAN and cannot be deleted.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no vlan access [<VLAN-ID>] command do?

The no vlan access [<VLAN-ID>] command creates an access interface and assigns an VLAN ID to it. Only one VLAN ID can be assigned to each access interface. VLANs can only be assigned to a non-routed (layer 2) interface or LAG interface. By default, all interfaces are routed (layer 3) when created. Use the no routing command to disable routing on an interface and change the interface to a layer 2 interface. The no form of this command removes an access VLAN from the interface in the current context and sets it to the default VLAN ID of 1.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no vlan trunk allowed [<VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The no vlan trunk allowed [<VLAN-LIST>] command assigns a VLAN ID to an trunk interface. Multiple VLAN IDs can be assigned to a trunk interface. These VLAN IDs define which VLAN traffic is allowed across the trunk interface. VLANs can be assigned only to a non-routed (layer 2) interface or LAG interface. By default, all interfaces are routed (layer 3) when created. Use the no routing command to disable routing on an interface. The no form of this command removes one or more VLAN IDs from a trunk interface. When the last VLAN is removed from a trunk interface, the interface continues to operate in trunk mode, and will trunk all the VLANs currently defined on the switch, and any new VLANs defined in the future. To disable the trunk interface, use the command shutdown.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no vlan trunk native [<VLAN-ID>] command do?

The no vlan trunk native [<VLAN-ID>] command assigns a native VLAN ID to a trunk interface. By default, VLAN ID 1 is assigned as the native VLAN ID for all trunk interfaces. VLANs can only be assigned to a non-routed (layer 2) interface or LAG interface. Only one VLAN ID can be assigned as the native VLAN. NOTE: When a native VLAN is defined, the switch automatically executes the vlan trunk allowed all command to ensure that the default VLAN is allowed on the trunk. To only allow specific VLANs on the trunk, issue the vlan trunk allowed command specifying only specific VLANs. The no form of this command removes a native VLAN from a trunk interface and assigns VLAN ID 1 as its native VLAN.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the no vlan trunk native <VLAN-ID> tag command do?

The no vlan trunk native <VLAN-ID> tag command enables tagging on a native VLAN. Only incoming packets that are tagged with the matching VLAN ID are accepted. Incoming packets that are untagged are dropped except for BPDUs. Egress packets are tagged. The no form of this command removes tagging on a native VLAN.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the voice command do?
The voice command configures a VLAN as a voice VLAN. The no form of this command removes voice configuration from a VLAN. Syntax: voice.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the voice command?
Syntax: voice
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Configuration and firmware management?

Configuration and firmware management: Checkpoints A checkpoint is a snapshot of the running configuration of a switch and its relevant metadata during the time of creation. Checkpoints can be used to apply the switch configuration stored within a checkpoint whenever needed, such as to revert to a previous, clean configuration. Checkpoints can be applied to other switches of the same platform. A switch is able to store multiple checkpoints. Checkpoint types The switch supports two types of checkpoints: • System generated checkpoints: The switch automatically generates a system checkpoint whenever a configuration change occurs. • User generated checkpoints: The administrator can manually generate a checkpoint whenever required. Maximum number of checkpoints • Maximum checkpoints: 64 (including the startup configuration) • Maximum user checkpoints: 32 • Maximum system checkpoints: 32 User generated checkpoints User checkpoints can be created at any time, as long as one configuration difference exists since the last checkpoint was created.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the checkpoint auto command do?

The checkpoint auto command starts auto checkpoint mode. In auto checkpoint mode, the switch temporarily saves the runtime configuration as a checkpoint only for the specified time lapse interval. Configuration changes must be saved before the interval expires, otherwise the runtime configuration is restored from the temporary checkpoint. Syntax: checkpoint auto <TIME-LAPSE-INTERVAL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint auto command?
Syntax: checkpoint auto <TIME-LAPSE-INTERVAL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the checkpoint auto confirm command do?

The checkpoint auto confirm command signals to the switch to save the running configuration used during the auto checkpoint mode. This command also ends the auto checkpoint mode. Syntax: checkpoint auto confirm.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint auto confirm command?
Syntax: checkpoint auto confirm
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the checkpoint diff command do?

The checkpoint diff command shows the difference in configuration between two configurations. Compare checkpoints, the running configuration, or the startup configuration. Syntax: checkpoint diff {<CHECKPOINT-NAME1> | running-config | startup-config}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint diff command?
Syntax: checkpoint diff {<CHECKPOINT-NAME1> | running-config | startup-config}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint post-configuration timeout command?
Syntax: checkpoint post-configuration timeout <TIMEOUT>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the checkpoint rename command do?
The checkpoint rename command renames an existing checkpoint. Syntax: checkpoint rename <OLD-CHECKPOINT-NAME> <NEW-CHECKPOINT-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint rename command?
Syntax: checkpoint rename <OLD-CHECKPOINT-NAME> <NEW-CHECKPOINT-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the checkpoint rollback command do?

The checkpoint rollback command applies the configuration from a pre-existing checkpoint or the startup configuration to the running configuration. Syntax: checkpoint rollback {<CHECKPOINT-NAME> | startup-config}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the checkpoint rollback command?
Syntax: checkpoint rollback {<CHECKPOINT-NAME> | startup-config}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> <REMOTE-URL> command do?

The copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> <REMOTE-URL> command copies a checkpoint configuration to a remote location as a file. The configuration is exported in checkpoint format, which includes switch configuration and relevant metadata. Syntax: copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> <REMOTE-URL> command?
Syntax: copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> {running-config startup-config} command?
Syntax: copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> {running-config | startup-config}
