The no form of this command removes the specified schedule profile from an interface and the interface uses the global schedule profile. This is the only way to remove a schedule profile override from the interface. NOTE: Interfaces may shut down briefly during reconfiguration. Syntax: apply qos [queue-profile <QUEUE-NAME>] schedule-profile <SCHEDULE-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the apply qos command?
Syntax: apply qos [queue-profile <QUEUE-NAME>] schedule-profile <SCHEDULE-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the min-bandwidth queue <QUEUE-NUMBER> percent <VALUE> command do?

The min-bandwidth queue <QUEUE-NUMBER> percent <VALUE> command assigns the Guaranteed Minimum Bandwidth (GMB) algorithm and a percentage of bandwidth to a queue. GMB allocates available bandwidth among all non-empty queues in relation to their configured minimum bandwidth. Non-empty queues are serviced first in strict order up to their minimum bandwidth. If there is any remaining bandwidth the scheduler will strictly service any remaining non-empty queues. The no form of this command only clears the algorithm for a queue if GMB has been assigned. Syntax: min-bandwidth queue <QUEUE-NUMBER> percent <VALUE>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the min-bandwidth queue <QUEUE-NUMBER> percent <VALUE> command?
Syntax: min-bandwidth queue <QUEUE-NUMBER> percent <VALUE>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the qos cos command do?

The qos cos command configures a CoS PCP remark for an Ethernet or LAG interface. Packets that ingress on the interface are remarked at egress using the configured CoS PCP value. The remark only occurs when QoS trust mode on the interface is set to none. 22 AOS-CX 10.06 Quality of Service Guide If QoS trust mode is not set to none, then the remark is ignored, and the following commands will show the CoS remark status as ignored (incompatible Port Access Trust configuration) or not applied' (incompatible QoS global/port Trust configuration): • show running-configuration • show interface <PORT-NUM> • show interface <PORT-NUM> qos The no form of this command removes a CoS remark on an interface. Syntax: qos cos <CODE-POINT>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the qos cos command?
Syntax: qos cos <CODE-POINT>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the qos dscp command do?

The qos dscp command configures a differentiated services code point (DSCP) remark for an Ethernet or LAG interface. IPv4 and IPv6 packets that ingress on the interface are remarked at egress using the configured DSCP value. The remark only occurs when QoS trust mode on the interface is set to none. If a DSCP remark is configured and then trust mode is subsequently set to cos or dscp, then the DSCP remark is ignored. The following commands will show the remark status as ignored (incompatible Port Access Trust configuration) or not applied' (incompatible QoS global/port Trust configuration):. Syntax: qos dscp <CODE-POINT>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the qos dscp command?
Syntax: qos dscp <CODE-POINT>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the qos queue-profile command do?

The qos queue-profile command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. A queue profile named factory-default is defined by default and is automatically applied to all interfaces. It cannot be edited or deleted. Use the command show qos queue-profile factory- default to view this profile. Do not use show running-configuration, as it will only display changes from the initial values. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Syntax: qos queue-profile <NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the qos queue-profile command?
Syntax: qos queue-profile <NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the qos schedule-profile command do?

The qos schedule-profile command creates a QoS schedule profile and switches to the config-schedule context for the profile. If the specified schedule profile exists, this command switches to the config-schedule context for the profile. The schedule profile determines the order in which queues are selected to transmit a packet, and the amount of service defined for each queue. Queues in a schedule profile are numbered consecutively starting from zero. Queue zero is the lowest priority queue. The larger the queue number, the higher priority the queue has in scheduling algorithms. A profile named factory-default is defined by default and applied to all interfaces. It cannot be edited or deleted. Syntax: qos schedule-profile <NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the qos schedule-profile command?
Syntax: qos schedule-profile <NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the qos trust command do?

The qos trust command configures one of three modes that are applied globally on all Ethernet interfaces and LAGs that have not applied their own trust mode. The QoS mode determines how CoS values are assigned to packets. In the config context: • This command sets the trust mode that is globally applied to all interfaces that do not have a trust mode configured. • The no form of this command restores all interfaces that do not currently have a trust mode configured to the default setting. 28 AOS-CX 10.06 Quality of Service Guide In the config-if context: • This command sets the trust mode override for a specific interface. • The no form of this command clears a trust mode override. The interface then uses the global setting. This is the only way to remove a trust mode override. Syntax: qos trust {none | cos | dscp}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the qos trust command?
Syntax: qos trust {none | cos | dscp}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show interface queues command do?
The show interface queues command shows the traffic transmitted per queue and the number of packets dropped due to the queue being full. Syntax: show interface <INTERFACE-NAME> queues [vsx-peer].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show interface queues command?
Syntax: show interface <INTERFACE-NAME> queues [vsx-peer]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show interface qos command do?
The show interface qos command shows various QoS settings for a specific interface. Syntax: show interface <INTERFACE-NAME> qos.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show interface qos command?
Syntax: show interface <INTERFACE-NAME> qos
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show qos dscp-map command do?
The show qos dscp-map command displays the current or default global QoS dscp-map. Syntax: show qos dscp-map [default].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show qos dscp-map command?
Syntax: show qos dscp-map [default]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show qos queue-profile command do?
The show qos queue-profile command shows the status of all queue profiles, or a specific queue profile. Syntax: show qos queue-profile [<NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show qos queue-profile command?
Syntax: show qos queue-profile [<NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show qos schedule-profile command do?
The show qos schedule-profile command is documented in this guide. Syntax: show qos schedule-profile [<NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show qos schedule-profile command?
Syntax: show qos schedule-profile [<NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show qos trust command?
Syntax: show qos trust [default]

section: Introduction to the AOS-CX REST API

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Introduction to the AOS-CX REST API?

Introduction to the AOS-CX REST API: NOTE: The Aruba 6100 Switch Series only supports the default VRF and has no management port. Therefore, references in this guide to other VRFs or the management port do no apply to the 6100 Switch Series. Configuration for these switches should be done over an SVI having a physical port with access to the SVI, since the physical ports in the 6100 are not routed. AOS-CX REST API Switches running the AOS-CX software are fully programmable with a REST (REpresentational State Transfer) API, allowing easy integration with other devices both on premises and in the cloud. This programmability- combined with the Aruba Network Analytics Engine-accelerates network administrator understanding of and response to network issues. The AOS-CX REST API is a web service that performs operations on switch resources using HTTPS POST, GET, PUT, and DELETE methods. The AOS-CX REST API enables programmatic access to the AOS-CX configuration and state database at the heart of the switch.

topic: Introduction to the AOS-CX REST API

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Configuration attributes?

Configuration attributes: represent user-owned data. Although an attribute must be in the configuration category to be modified by a user, not all attributes in the configuration category can be modified after the resource instance is created. Configuration attributes that cannot be changed after the resource is created are called immutable attributes. This distinction matters when using a PUT request, because immutable attributes cannot be included in the request body. For example, a VLAN ID is an immutable attribute. You cannot change the ID of the VLAN after the VLAN is created. The VLAN name, in contrast, is a mutable (writable) attribute. You can change the VLAN name after the VLAN is created. Writable attributes Writable attributes are the subset of configuration attributes that are mutable. Writable attributes can be modified by a user after the resource is created. When using the PUT method to modify a resource, only writable attributes can be included in the request body.

section: Enabling access to the REST API

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what REST API endpoint is documented for POST /rest/v1/login? username=admin?

The REST API reference documents POST /rest/v1/login? username=admin. Details: The AOS-CX Web UI and AOS-CX real-time notifications subsystem rely on the REST API, therefore, all three are enabled or disabled together.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the https-server rest access-mode command do?
The https-server rest access-mode command changes the REST API access mode. The default mode is read-write. Syntax: https-server rest access-mode {read-only | read-write}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the https-server rest access-mode command?
Syntax: https-server rest access-mode {read-only | read-write}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the https-server session close all command do?

The https-server session close all command invalidates and closes all HTTPS sessions. All existing Web UI and REST sessions are logged out and all real- time notification feature Web Socket connections are closed. Syntax: https-server session close all.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the https-server session close all command?
Syntax: https-server session close all
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the https-server vrf command do?

The https-server vrf command configures and starts the HTTPS server on the specified VRF. HTTPS server features include the REST API and the web user interfaces. The no form of the command stops any HTTPS servers running on the specified VRF and removes the HTTPS server configuration. Syntax: https-server vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the https-server vrf command?
Syntax: https-server vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show https-server command do?

The show https-server command shows the status and configuration of the HTTPS server. The REST API and web user interface are accessible only on VRFs that have the HTTPS server features configured. Syntax: show https-server [vsx-peer].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show https-server command?
Syntax: show https-server [vsx-peer]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VLAN interfaces?

VLAN interfaces: • VLAN interfaces are of type vlan. • You can use the DELETE method to delete a VLAN interface. Example of creating a VLAN interface: Method and URI: POST "/rest/v1/system/interfaces" Request body: { "name": "vlan2", "vlan_tag": "/rest/v1/system/vlans/2", "vrf": "/rest/v1/system/vrfs/default", "type": "vlan" } Write methods (POST, PUT) supported in read-only mode The following switch resources support write methods (POST, PUT, or both) even when the REST API access mode is set to read-only:
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Configuration maximums?

Configuration maximums: • Maximum number of subscribers per switch: 50 • Maximum number of subscriptions per subscriber: 80 • Maximum number of topics in one subscription message: eight Enabling the notifications subsystem on a switch The AOS-CX real-time notifications subsystem relies on the REST API, so the REST API must be enabled on the switch and VRF from which you want to receive notifications. HTTPS server must be enabled on the specified VRF. The VRF you specify determines from which network the HTTPS server can be accessed. You can enable access on multiple VRFs, including user-defined VRFs. Procedure Enable REST API access on the VRF from which you will access the switch. Establishing a secure Web Socket connection through a web browser Prerequisites • Access to the switch REST API must be enabled. The REST API access mode can be either read-only or read/write. • The web browser you use must support the secure Web Socket Protocol. Procedure 1. Open a web browser page and log in to the switch Web UI or the REST API.

section: Enabling access to the REST API

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what REST API endpoint is documented for POST /rest/v10.04/login? username=admin?

The REST API reference documents POST /rest/v10.04/login? username=admin. Details: The AOS-CX Web UI and AOS-CX real-time notifications subsystem rely on the REST API, therefore, all three are enabled or disabled together.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for About security?

About security: This AOS-CX Switch provides the following security features: • Local user and group management. • Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA), either local (password or SSH public key-based), or remote password-based TACACS+ or RADIUS. • SSH server. SSH is a cryptographic protocol that encrypts all communication between devices. • Ability to use enhanced security as described in Configuring enhanced security. • Making sensitive switch configuration information available for secure export/import between switches. For information, see service export-password. About Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA) • Authentication: identifies users, validates their credentials, and grants switch access. • Authorization: controls authenticated users command execution and switch interaction privileges. • Accounting: collects and manages user session activity logs for auditing and reporting purposes. Local AAA on your Aruba switch provides: • Authentication using local password or SSH public key.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for Managing local users and groups?

Managing local users and groups: Default user admin A factory-default switch comes with a single user named admin. The admin user: • Has an empty password. Press Enter in response to the admin password prompt. At initial boot, you are prompted to define a password for the admin user. Although empty (blank) passwords are allowed, it is recommended that you use strong passwords for all production switches. • Is a member of the administrators group. • Cannot be removed from the switch. NOTE: The switch admin user is distinct from the Service OS admin user. The Service OS acts as the bootloader and recovery operating system. The Service OS has its own CLI.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the user <USERNAME> group {administrators | operators | auditors | <USER-GROUP>} command do?

The user <USERNAME> group {administrators | operators | auditors | <USER-GROUP>} command creates a user and adds the user to one of the user groups. Users are given the privileges of their group. For the three built-in user groups (administrators, operators, auditors), the privileges are fixed. For user- defined local user groups, the privileges are defined by the CLI command authorization rules of the group. When entered without either optional ciphertext or plaintext parameters, the cleartext password is prompted for twice, with the characters entered masked with "*" symbols. The no form of this command removes a user account from the switch. The administrator cannot delete the user account from which they are logged in. The admin user cannot be deleted. 20. Syntax: user <USERNAME> group {administrators | operators | auditors | <USER-GROUP>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the user <USERNAME> group {administrators | operators | auditors | <USER-GROUP>} command?
Syntax: user <USERNAME> group {administrators | operators | auditors | <USER-GROUP>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the user-group <GROUP-NAME> command do?

The user-group <GROUP-NAME> command if <GROUP-NAME> does not exist, this command creates a local user group and then enters its context. If <GROUP-NAME> exists, this command enters the context for the specified <GROUP-NAME>. Within the <GROUP-NAME> context, several subcommands are available for working with rules that specify what CLI commands are permitted or denied for all members of the local group. In addition to the three built-in user groups administrators, operators, and auditors, up to 29 user- defined local user groups can be defined. All users can be members of only one of the up to 32 groups. The no form of this command deletes the specified user group. All members of the deleted group lose all command authorization privilege. Syntax: user-group <GROUP-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the user-group <GROUP-NAME> command?
Syntax: user-group <GROUP-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the user <USERNAME> password [ciphertext <CIPHERTEXT-PASSWORD> | plaintext <PLAINTEXT-PASSWORD>] command do?

The user <USERNAME> password [ciphertext <CIPHERTEXT-PASSWORD> | plaintext <PLAINTEXT-PASSWORD>] command changes a password for an account or enables the password for the admin account. When entered without either optional ciphertext or plaintext parameters, the cleartext password is prompted for twice, with the characters entered masked with "*" symbols. Syntax: user <USERNAME> password [ciphertext <CIPHERTEXT-PASSWORD> | plaintext <PLAINTEXT-PASSWORD>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the user <USERNAME> password [ciphertext <CIPHERTEXT-PASSWORD> | plaintext <PLAINTEXT-PASSWORD>] command?
Syntax: user <USERNAME> password [ciphertext <CIPHERTEXT-PASSWORD> | plaintext <PLAINTEXT-PASSWORD>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the service export-password command do?

The service export-password command configures a nondefault export password. The export password is used to transform critical security parameters (such as password hashes) into ciphertext suitable for exporting and showing by commands such as show running-config. This transformation enables safe switch configuration import and export. The no form of this command reverts the export password to its factory default. NOTE: All factory-default switches have identical default export passwords. For security, it is recommended that you set the same nondefault export password on every switch in a group that will exchange configuration information. Only switches with identical export passwords can exchange configuration information. Syntax: service export-password.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the service export-password command?
Syntax: service export-password
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show user-group command do?

The show user-group command shows user group information for the built-in groups plus any user-defined local user groups. When entered without <GROUP-NAME>, summary information is shown for all groups. Syntax: show user-group [<GROUP-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show user-group command?
Syntax: show user-group [<GROUP-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show user information command do?

The show user information command shows the following information for the logged-in user: • User name. • User authentication type: local, RADIUS, or TACACS+. • User group: administrators, operators, or <GROUP-NAME>. • User privilege level: For the built-in user groups and RADIUS or TACACS+, the role privilege level value is shown. For user-defined user groups, N/A is shown. Syntax: show user information.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show user information command?
Syntax: show user information
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show user-list command do?
The show user-list command shows all configured users and their corresponding group names. Syntax: show user-list.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show user-list command?
Syntax: show user-list
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for SSH server?

SSH server: About the SSH server NOTE: On the 6100 Switch Series, only the vrf named default is available. Replace any references to the mgmt or other VRFs with default. SSH (Secure Shell) is a cryptographic protocol that encrypts all communication between devices. Each switch VRF includes an SSH server. The SSH server on the mgmt VRF is enabled by default in software version 10.02 and higher, and disabled in version 10.01 and lower. Only the SSH servers included in the switch are supported. The SSH server provides SSH client to switch communications, enabling SSH clients (at least SSH v2.0) to connect to the switch for the purpose of managing it. The SSH server interfaces with the authentication service that provides local and/or remote AAA. NOTE: The SSH server will perform a rekey operation for all open SSH sessions at every hour or after 1 GB of data transferred, whichever occurs first. The rekey is performed to address a common security concern that encryption/decryption keys not be used for long periods of time.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ssh server command do?
The show ssh server command shows the SSH server configuration for the default VRF. 34. Syntax: show ssh server [vrf default].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ssh server command?
Syntax: show ssh server [vrf default]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ssh server sessions command do?
The show ssh server sessions command shows the active SSH sessions on the default VRF. Syntax: show ssh server sessions [vrf default].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ssh server sessions command?
Syntax: show ssh server sessions [vrf default]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh ciphers command do?

The ssh ciphers command configures SSH to use a set of ciphers in the specified priority order. Ciphers in SSH are used for privacy of data being transported over the connection. The first cipher type entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. Each option is an algorithm that is used to encrypt the link and each name indicates the algorithm and cryptographic parameters that are used. Only ciphers that are entered by the user are configured. The no form of this command removes the configuration of ciphers and reverts SSH to use the default set of ciphers. Syntax: ssh ciphers <CIPHERS-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh ciphers command?
Syntax: ssh ciphers <CIPHERS-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh host-key-algorithms command do?

The ssh host-key-algorithms command configures SSH to use a set of host key algorithms in the specified priority order. Host key algorithms specify which host key types are allowed to be used for the SSH connection. The first host key entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. Each option represents a type of key that can be used. Host keys are used to verify the host that you are connecting to. This configuration allows you to control which host key types are presented to incoming clients, or which host key types to receive first from hosts. Only the host key algorithms that are specified by the user are configured. 38. Syntax: ssh host-key-algorithms <HOST-KEY-ALGORITHMS-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh host-key-algorithms command?
Syntax: ssh host-key-algorithms <HOST-KEY-ALGORITHMS-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh key-exchange-algorithms command do?

The ssh key-exchange-algorithms command configures SSH to use a set of key exchange algorithm types in the specified priority order. The first key exchange type entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. Key exchange algorithms are used to exchange a shared session key with a peer securely. Each option represents an algorithm that is used to distribute a shared key in a way that prevents outside interference, manipulation, or recovery. Only the key exchange algorithms that are specified by the user are configured. The no form of this command removes the configuration of key exchange algorithms and reverts SSH to use the default set of algorithms. Syntax: ssh key-exchange-algorithms <KEY-EXCHANGE-ALGORITHMS-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh key-exchange-algorithms command?
Syntax: ssh key-exchange-algorithms <KEY-EXCHANGE-ALGORITHMS-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh known-host remove command do?

The ssh known-host remove command clears the list of trusted SSH servers for your user account. When you download or upload a file to or from a server using SFTP, you establish a trusted SSH relationship with that server. Each user account maintains its own set of SSH server host-keys for every server to which the user previously connected. Syntax: ssh known-host remove {all | {<IPv4-ADDRESS> | <HOSTNAME> | <IPv6-ADDRESS>} }.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh known-host remove command?
Syntax: ssh known-host remove {all | {<IPv4-ADDRESS> | <HOSTNAME> | <IPv6-ADDRESS>} }
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh macs command do?

The ssh macs command configures SSH to use a set of message authentication codes (MAC s) in the specified priority order. The first MAC entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. MAC s maintain the integrity of each message sent across an SSH connection. Each option represents an algorithm that can be used to provide integrity between peers. Only the MAC types that are specified by the user are configured. The no form of this command removes the configuration of MAC s and reverts SSH to use the default set of MAC s. Syntax: ssh macs <MACS-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh macs command?
Syntax: ssh macs <MACS-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh maximum-auth-attempts command do?

The ssh maximum-auth-attempts command sets the SSH maximum number of authentication attempts. The no form of the command resets the maximum to its default of 6. Syntax: ssh maximum-auth-attempts <ATTEMPTS>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh maximum-auth-attempts command?
Syntax: ssh maximum-auth-attempts <ATTEMPTS>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh public-key-algorithms command do?

The ssh public-key-algorithms command configures SSH to use a set of public key algorithms in the specified priority order. The first public key type entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. Public key algorithms specify which public key types can be used for public key authentication in SSH. Each option represents a public key type that the SSH server can accept or that the SSH client can present to a server. Only the public key algorithms that are chosen by the user are configured. The no form of this command removes the configuration of public key algorithms and reverts SSH to use the default set. Syntax: ssh public-key-algorithms <PUBLIC-KEY-ALGORITHMS-LIST>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh public-key-algorithms command?
Syntax: ssh public-key-algorithms <PUBLIC-KEY-ALGORITHMS-LIST>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh server vrf command do?
The ssh server vrf command enables the SSH server on the specified VRF. The no form of the command disables the SSH server on the specified VRF. Syntax: ssh server vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh server vrf command?
Syntax: ssh server vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh (client login) command do?
The ssh (client login) command establishes a client session with an SSH server which is typically another switch. Syntax: ssh [<USERNAME>@]{<IPv4> | <HOSTNAME>} [vrf <VRF-NAME>] [port <PORT-NUMBER>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh (client login) command?
Syntax: ssh [<USERNAME>@]{<IPv4> | <HOSTNAME>} [vrf <VRF-NAME>] [port <PORT-NUMBER>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for Local AAA?

Local AAA: About local AAA Local AAA on your Aruba switch provides: • Authentication using local password or SSH public key. • Authorization using local role-based access control (RBAC). Optional per-command authorization is possible through configuration of user-defined local user groups, with command authorization rules applied to respective group members. • Accounting of user activity on the switch using accounting logs. NOTE: For switches that support multiple management modules such as the Aruba 8400, all AAA functionality discussed only applies to the active management module. See also AAA on switches with multiple management modules in the High Availability Guide. Local AAA defaults and limits Setting Default value / limit Local authentication Enabled by default for all connection types: console, SSH, and REST. Local role-based access control (RBAC) authorization Enabled by default for all connection types: console, SSH, and REST. Local accounting Enabled. Maximum number of local users 64 users, including the default admin user.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa accounting all-mgmt command do?

The aaa accounting all-mgmt command defines accounting as being local (with the name local) (the default). Or defines a sequence of remote AAA server groups to be accessed for accounting purposes. For remote accounting, the information is sent to the first reachable remote server that was configured with this command for remote accounting. If no remote server is reachable, local accounting remains available. Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually as either local or using remote AAA server groups. All server groups named in your command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. Local is always available for any connection type not configured for remote accounting. Syntax: aaa accounting all-mgmt <CONNECTION-TYPE> start-stop {local | group <GROUP-LIST>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa accounting all-mgmt command?
Syntax: aaa accounting all-mgmt <CONNECTION-TYPE> start-stop {local | group <GROUP-LIST>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication limit-login-attempts command do?

The aaa authentication limit-login-attempts command enables local login attempt limiting. If the number of failed local login attempts equals the configured threshold, the user is locked out for the configured duration. The no form of this command disables local login attempt limits. NOTE: This local login attempt limiting feature is only available when not using remote authentication through AAA servers (TACACS+ or RADIUS). Syntax: aaa authentication limit-login-attempts <ATTEMPTS> lockout-time <LOCKOUT-TIME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication limit-login-attempts command?
Syntax: aaa authentication limit-login-attempts <ATTEMPTS> lockout-time <LOCKOUT-TIME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication login command do?

The aaa authentication login command defines authentication as being local (with the name local) (the default). Or defines a sequence of remote AAA server groups to be accessed for authentication purposes. Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually as either local or using remote AAA server groups. All server groups named in your command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. Local is always available for any connection type not configured for remote AAA authentication. The no form of this command removes for the specified connection type, any defined remote AAA server group authentication sequence. Syntax: aaa authentication login <CONNECTION-TYPE> {local | group <GROUP-LIST>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication login command?
Syntax: aaa authentication login <CONNECTION-TYPE> {local | group <GROUP-LIST>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication minimum-password-length command do?

The aaa authentication minimum-password-length command enables minimum password length checking. Existing passwords shorter than the minimum length are unaffected. Length checking does not apply to ciphertext passwords. Length checking applies both to local and remote authentication. The no form of this command disables minimum password length checking. Syntax: aaa authentication minimum-password-length <LENGTH>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication minimum-password-length command?
Syntax: aaa authentication minimum-password-length <LENGTH>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show aaa accounting command do?
The show aaa accounting command shows the accounting configuration per connection type (channel). Syntax: show aaa accounting.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show aaa accounting command?
Syntax: show aaa accounting
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show aaa authentication command do?
The show aaa authentication command shows the authentication configuration per connection type (channel). Syntax: show aaa authentication.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show aaa authentication command?
Syntax: show aaa authentication
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show aaa authorization command do?
The show aaa authorization command shows the authorization configuration per connection type (channel). Syntax: show aaa authorization.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show aaa authorization command?
Syntax: show aaa authorization
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ssh authentication-method command do?

The show ssh authentication-method command shows the status of the SSH public key method and the local password-based (through SSH client) authentication method. Syntax: show ssh authentication-method.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ssh authentication-method command?
Syntax: show ssh authentication-method
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show user command do?
The show user command shows the SSH client public key list for a specified user. Syntax: show user <USERNAME> authorized-key.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show user command?
Syntax: show user <USERNAME> authorized-key
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ssh public-key-authentication command do?

The ssh public-key-authentication command enables the SSH public key authentication method. The switch ships with SSH public key authentication enabled. The no form of this command disables the SSH public key authentication method. NOTE: Although SSH public key authentication is enabled by default, it cannot be used until SSH public keys are added with the user authorized-key command. Syntax: ssh public-key-authentication.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ssh public-key-authentication command?
Syntax: ssh public-key-authentication
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for Remote AAA with TACACS+?

Remote AAA with TACACS+: About remote AAA with TACACS+ NOTE: On the 6100 Switch Series, only the vrf named default is available. Replace any references to the mgmt or other VRFs with default. Remote AAA provides the following for your Aruba switch: • Authentication using remote TACACS+ AAA servers. • Authorization using remote TACACS+ AAA servers, providing fine-grained command authorization. Optional user-defined local user groups with configured command authorization rules can be used to provide authorization fallback protection for when TACACS+ servers become temporarily unavailable. • Transmission of locally collected accounting information to remote TACACS+ servers. NOTE: For switches that support multiple management modules such as the Aruba 8400, all AAA functionality discussed only applies to the active management module. See also AAA on switches with multiple management modules in the High Availability Guide. Default server groups The switch always has these four default groups: • tacacs: for remote AAA, always contains every configured TACACS+ server.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for Remote AAA with RADIUS?

Remote AAA with RADIUS: About remote AAA with RADIUS NOTE: On the 6100 Switch Series, only the vrf named default is available. Replace any references to the mgmt or other VRFs with default. Remote AAA provides the following for your Aruba switch: • Authentication using remote RADIUS AAA servers. For added security, two-factor authentication may be used. In two-factor authentication, X.509 certificate-based authentication is combined with RADIUS authentication. • Command authorization is not supported by RADIUS servers, however, user-defined local user groups can be configured with command-authorization rules, providing locally configured per-command authorization for members of such groups. See User-defined user groups. In the switch default state (without user-defined local groups), basic role-based authorization is available with the three built-in roles (administrators, operators, auditors). • Transmission of locally collected accounting information to remote RADIUS servers.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for Remote AAA (TACACS+, RADIUS) commands?

Remote AAA (TACACS+, RADIUS) commands: NOTE: On the 6100 Switch Series, only the vrf named default is available. Replace any references to the mgmt or other VRFs with default. aaa accounting all-mgmt Syntax aaa accounting all-mgmt <CONNECTION-TYPE> start-stop {local | group <GROUP-LIST>} no aaa accounting all-mgmt <CONNECTION-TYPE> Description Defines accounting as being local (with the name local) (the default). Or defines a sequence of remote AAA server groups to be accessed for accounting purposes. For remote accounting, the information is sent to the first reachable remote server that was configured with this command for remote accounting. If no remote server is reachable, local accounting remains available. Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually as either local or using remote AAA server groups. All server groups named in your command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. Local is always available for any connection type not configured for remote accounting.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication allow-fail-through command do?

The aaa authentication allow-fail-through command enables authentication fail-through. When this option is enabled, the next server/authentication method is tried after an authentication failure. The no form of this command disables authentication fail-through. If the system fails to authenticate with a reachable TACACS+ or RADIUS server, the system does not attempt to authenticate with the next TACACS+/ RADIUS server. Syntax: aaa authentication allow-fail-through.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication allow-fail-through command?
Syntax: aaa authentication allow-fail-through
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa group server command do?

The aaa group server command creates an AAA server group that is either empty or contains preconfigured RADIUS/TACACS+ servers. You can create a maximum of 28 server groups. The no form of this command deletes a server group. Only a preconfigured user-defined RADIUS/TACACS+ server group can be deleted. RADIUS or TACACS+ servers that were in a deleted server group remain a part 94. Syntax: aaa group server {tacacs | radius} <SERVER-GROUP-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa group server command?
Syntax: aaa group server {tacacs | radius} <SERVER-GROUP-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius-server auth-type command do?

The radius-server auth-type command enables the CHAP or PAP authentication protocol, which is used for communication with the RADIUS servers, at the global level. You can override this command with a fine-grained per server auth-type configuration. The no form of this command resets the global authentication mechanism for RADIUS to PAP, which is the default authentication mechanism for RADIUS. Syntax: radius-server auth-type {pap | chap}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius-server auth-type command?
Syntax: radius-server auth-type {pap | chap}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius-server key command do?

The radius-server key command creates or modifies a RADIUS global passkey. The RADIUS global passkey is used as a shared-secret for encrypting the communication between all RADIUS servers and the switch. The RADIUS global passkey is required for authentication unless local passkeys have been set. By default, the RADIUS global passkey is empty. If the administrator has not set this key, the switch will not be able to perform RADIUS authentication. The switch will instead rely on the authentication mechanism configured with aaa authentication login. The no form of the command removes the global passkey. Syntax: radius-server key {plaintext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY> | ciphertext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius-server key command?
Syntax: radius-server key {plaintext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY> | ciphertext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius-server retries command do?

The radius-server retries command sets at the global level the number of retries the switch makes before concluding that the RADIUS server is unreachable. You can override this setting with a fine-grained per RADIUS server retries configuration. See radius- server host. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS global retries to the default retries value of 1. Syntax: radius-server retries <0-5>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius-server retries command?
Syntax: radius-server retries <0-5>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius-server timeout command do?

The radius-server timeout command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the RADIUS server before trying the next RADIUS server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Syntax: radius-server timeout [<1-60>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius-server timeout command?
Syntax: radius-server timeout [<1-60>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command adds a TACACS+/RADIUS server to a server-group. Only the configured TACACS+/RADIUS servers are allowed to be added within the server group. If the same server name exists with multiple ports or multiple VRFs, specify the server name, port, and VRF when adding the server to the server-group. The no form of this command removes a TACACS+/RADIUS server from a server-group. Syntax: server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command?
Syntax: server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show aaa server-groups command do?
The show aaa server-groups command shows TACACS+ and RADIUS AAA server group information for all server types or for the specified server type. Syntax: show aaa server-groups [tacacs | radius].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show aaa server-groups command?
Syntax: show aaa server-groups [tacacs | radius]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show accounting log command do?

The show accounting log command entered without optional parameters, this command shows all accounting log records for the current boot. Sensitive information is masked from the log, by being represented as asterisks. NOTE: This show accounting log command replaces the show audit-log command that is supported only in 10.00 releases. Syntax: show accounting log [last <QTY-TO-SHOW> | all].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show accounting log command?
Syntax: show accounting log [last <QTY-TO-SHOW> | all]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show radius-server command do?
The show radius-server command shows configured RADIUS servers information. Syntax: show radius-server [detail].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show radius-server command?
Syntax: show radius-server [detail]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show tacacs-server command do?
The show tacacs-server command shows the configured TACACS+ servers. Syntax: show tacacs-server [detail].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show tacacs-server command?
Syntax: show tacacs-server [detail]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show tacacs-server statistics command do?
The show tacacs-server statistics command shows authentication statistics for all configured TACACS+ servers. Syntax: show tacacs-server statistics.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show tacacs-server statistics command?
Syntax: show tacacs-server statistics
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show tech aaa command do?
The show tech aaa command is documented in this guide. Syntax: show tech aaa.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show tech aaa command?
Syntax: show tech aaa
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the tacacs-server timeout command do?

The tacacs-server timeout command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the TACACS+ server before trying the next TACACS+ server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the TACACS+ global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Syntax: tacacs-server timeout [<1-60>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the tacacs-server timeout command?
Syntax: tacacs-server timeout [<1-60>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for RADIUS dynamic authorization?

RADIUS dynamic authorization: About RADIUS dynamic authorization RADIUS dynamic authorization provides the ability to make changes to a user account session while it is in progress. This ability includes disconnecting a session or updating some aspect of the authorization for the session. It also includes "pot bounce" in which the interface on which a client is connected is brought down and then back up (using COA (change of authorization). RADIUS dynamic authorization enables or disables the processing of "Disconnect" and "Change of Authorization (CoA)" messages from the RADIUS server. When enabled, the RADIUS server can dynamically terminate or change the authorization parameters (such as VLAN/user-role assignment) used in an active client session on the switch. NOTE: See also RFC 3576 available at http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3576.txt for general information on the dynamic authorization extensions to RADIUS.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius dyn-authorization enable command do?

The radius dyn-authorization enable command enables RADIUS dynamic authorization. This command must be issued before the configuration set with other radius dyn-authorization commands takes effect. The no form of this command disables RADIUS dynamic authorization. Syntax: radius dyn-authorization enable.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius dyn-authorization enable command?
Syntax: radius dyn-authorization enable
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius dyn-authorization client command do?

The radius dyn-authorization client command configures RADIUS dynamic authorization for the specified client on the specified (or default) VRF. The no form of this command unconfigures RADIUS dynamic authorization for the specified client on the specified (or default) VRF. Syntax: radius dyn-authorization client {<IPv4> | <IPv6> | <HOSTNAME>}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius dyn-authorization client command?
Syntax: radius dyn-authorization client {<IPv4> | <IPv6> | <HOSTNAME>}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius dyn-authorization port command do?
The radius dyn-authorization port command sets the RADIUS dynamic authorization server UDP/TCP port. Syntax: radius dyn-authorization port <PORT-NUMBER>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius dyn-authorization port command?
Syntax: radius dyn-authorization port <PORT-NUMBER>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show radius dyn-authorization command do?

The show radius dyn-authorization command shows RADIUS dynamic authorization configuration and summarized statistics for all clients configured for dynamic authorization. Syntax: show radius dyn-authorization.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show radius dyn-authorization command?
Syntax: show radius dyn-authorization
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show radius dyn-authorization client command do?

The show radius dyn-authorization client command shows RADIUS dynamic authorization statistics for the specified client on the specified VRF. Syntax: show radius dyn-authorization client <IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show radius dyn-authorization client command?
Syntax: show radius dyn-authorization client <IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for PKI?

PKI: The public key infrastructure (PKI) feature enables administrators to manage digital certificates on the switch. The switch uses certificates to validate SSH clients when acting as an SSH server, and when communicating with syslog servers when TLS encryption is used. NOTE: On the 6100 Switch Series, only the vrf named default is available. Replace any references to the mgmt or other VRFs with default. PKI concepts Digital certificate A digital certificate is an electronic form of identification that stores important information about an entity (such as a computer, program, or website). Certificates help secure digital transactions by enabling the end parties to validate each other's identity. Digital certificates are issued by a certificate authority (CA) and are composed of an encoded string of characters (usually stored in a file). For example: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- MIIDsDCCApgCCQDJotuPPj9GCDANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQsAADCBqzELMAkGA1UEBh VVMx EzARBgNVBAgMCk Nhb Glmb3JuaWExEDAOBgNVBAcBM1JvY2tsaW4xDDAKBg BAoMA0hQTjEVMBMGA1UECwwMSFBOUm9zZXZpb GxlMSokwAYDVQQDDCFocG5zdz...
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki application command do?

The crypto pki application command associates a leaf certificate with a feature (application) on the switch. By default, all features are associated with the default, self-signed certificate local-cert. This certificate is created by the switch the first time it starts. The no form of this command associates the specified feature with the default certificate. Syntax: crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki application command?
Syntax: crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki certificate command do?

The crypto pki certificate command creates a leaf certificate and changes to its context config-cert-<CERT-NAME>. If the specified leaf certificate exists, this command changes to its context. The first time the switch starts it creates a self-signed, default leaf certificate called local-cert. This certificate is used by any switch application that does not have an associated leaf certificate. The no form of this command deletes the specified leaf certificate. The default leaf certificate local-cert cannot be deleted. 154. Syntax: crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki certificate command?
Syntax: crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki ta-profile command do?

The crypto pki ta-profile command creates a trust anchor (TA) profile and changes to the config-ta-<TA-NAME> context for the profile. Each TA profile stores the certificate for a trusted CA. Up to 64 profiles can be defined. If the specified TA profile exists, this command changes to the config-ta-<TA-NAME> context for the profile. The no form of this command removes the specified TA profile. NOTE: When creating a new profile, If you exit the config-ta-<TA-NAME> context without importing the TA certificate, the profile is discarded. Syntax: crypto pki ta-profile <TA-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki ta-profile command?
Syntax: crypto pki ta-profile <TA-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the enroll self-signed command do?

The enroll self-signed command generates a key pair and generates a self-signed certificate with it. The subject fields and key type of the current leaf certificate must be defined before running this command. If not, you are prompted to fill in the subject fields, and the key type is set to RSA 2048. Generates a key pair and certificate signing request (CSR) for the current leaf certificate. Use the CSR to obtain a signed certificate from a certificate authority (CA), and then import the certificate onto the switch with the command import terminal. 156. Syntax: enroll self-signed.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the enroll self-signed command?
Syntax: enroll self-signed
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the import terminal ta-profile <TA-NAME> [password <PW>] command do?

The import terminal ta-profile <TA-NAME> [password <PW>] command imports a CA-signed leaf certificate and then validates the certificate against the specified TA profile. If the imported data includes a private key, the private key must match the leaf certificate being imported. If the imported data does not include a private key, the certificate must match a CSR that was previously generated with the command enroll terminal and must be signed by the CA whose root certificate is installed in the specified TA profile. The TA profile must exist and have a TA certificate configured. Syntax: import terminal ta-profile <TA-NAME> [password <PW>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the import terminal ta-profile <TA-NAME> [password <PW>] command?
Syntax: import terminal ta-profile <TA-NAME> [password <PW>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command do?
The import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command imports a self-signed leaf certificate including its matching private key. Syntax: import terminal self-signed [password <PW>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command?
Syntax: import terminal self-signed [password <PW>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command do?

The key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command sets the key type and key size for the current leaf certificate. The key type of the default certificate local- cert cannot be changed. Syntax: key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command?
Syntax: key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ocsp disable-nonce command do?

The ocsp disable-nonce command configures exclusion of the nonce from OCSP requests. A nonce is a unique identifier that an OCSP client inserts in an OCSP request and expects the OCSP responder to include it in the corresponding OCSP response. The nonce mechanism helps prevent replay attacks in which a malicious player attempts to masquerade as the OCSP responder. Although the nonce is included by default, it can be excluded. Some OCSP responders choose to not support the use of the nonce due to performance considerations. The no form of this command re-enables nonce inclusion in OCSP requests. Sets either strict or reduced enforcement of the OCSP check of certificates. Strict enforcement is enabled by default. The no form of this command resets enforcement to its default of strict. Syntax: ocsp disable-nonce.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ocsp disable-nonce command?
Syntax: ocsp disable-nonce
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ocsp url {primary | secondary} <URL> command do?

The ocsp url {primary | secondary} <URL> command configures the OCSP responder URLs that the current TA profile uses to verify the revocation status of an X.509 digital certificate. These URLs override the OCSP responder URL contained within the peer certificate being verified (as well as URLs defined in any intermediate CAs in the chain of trust). If no OCSP responder URLs are defined for a TA profile (default setting), then the OCSP responder URL in the peer certificate is used for revocation status checking. (The OCSP responder URL is contained in a certificate's Authority Information Access field, which is an X.509 v3 certificate extension.) The no form of this command deletes the specified OCSP responder URL (primary or secondary) from the current TA profile. Syntax: ocsp url {primary | secondary} <URL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ocsp url {primary | secondary} <URL> command?
Syntax: ocsp url {primary | secondary} <URL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command sets the VRF that the switch uses to communicate with OCSP responders for OCSP checking. VRF mgmt is used by default. The no form of this command resets the VRF to its default mgmt. Syntax: ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command?
Syntax: ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki application command do?
The show crypto pki application command shows certificate information for all features (applications) using leaf certificates that are managed by PKI. Syntax: show crypto pki application.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki application command?
Syntax: show crypto pki application
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki certificate command do?

The show crypto pki certificate command shows a list of all configured leaf certificates, or detailed information for a specific leaf certificate. Possible values for Cert Status are: CSR pending, expired, expires soon, installed, malformed, not yet known. Possible values for EST Status are: enroll failed, enroll pending, enroll retrying, enroll success, n/a (certificate is not EST-enrolled), reenroll failed, reenroll pending, reenroll retrying. Syntax: show crypto pki certificate [<CERT-NAME> [plaintext | pem]].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki certificate command?
Syntax: show crypto pki certificate [<CERT-NAME> [plaintext | pem]]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki ta-profile command do?

The show crypto pki ta-profile command shows a list of all configured TA profiles, or detailed information for a specific profile. NOTE: This command shows information for both directly-configured TA profiles and TA profiles that were dynamically downloaded from EST servers. Syntax: show crypto pki ta-profile [<TA-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki ta-profile command?
Syntax: show crypto pki ta-profile [<TA-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command do?

The ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command imports a CA certificate for use in the current TA profile. The certificate must be in PEM format. The PEM data must be delimited with these lines: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- -----END CERTIFICATE----- NOTE: Only the first certificate in the PEM data is imported. Any additional certificates are ignored. Syntax: ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} }.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command?
Syntax: ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} }
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command do?

The subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command sets the subject fields for the current leaf certificate. If the common-name parameter is not specified, then you are prompted to define a value for each field. If a configured value exists for any field, it is presented as the default. The subject fields of the default certificate local-cert cannot be changed. Syntax: subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command?
Syntax: subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the arbitrary-label <LABEL> command do?

The arbitrary-label <LABEL> command within the EST profile context, configures the generic optional label (also known as arbitrary label) to be concatenated to the EST server URL that is configured with the url command. There is no arbitrary label configured by default. Any existing arbitrary label is replaced by this command. The use of arbitrary labels is optional. RFC 7030 allows the use of arbitrary labels so that one EST server may serve multiple CAs with the same server URL that gets concatenated with different arbitrary labels. The same label is used for every request made under a particular EST profile. Some EST schemes use arbitrary labels in a more sophisticated way, defining different labels for different types of requests under the same EST profile. Syntax: arbitrary-label <LABEL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the arbitrary-label <LABEL> command?
Syntax: arbitrary-label <LABEL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command do?

The arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command within the EST profile context, configures the arbitrary enrollment label to be concatenated to the EST server URL that is configured with the url command. This label is specific to the enrollment operation. There is no arbitrary enrollment label configured by default. Any existing arbitrary enrollment label is replaced by this command. The use of arbitrary enrollment labels is optional. When the enrollment label is not configured, the generic arbitrary label (created with the arbitrary-label command) is used (if configured) for enrollment. RFC 7030 allows the use of arbitrary labels so that one EST server may serve multiple CAs with the same server URL that gets concatenated with different arbitrary labels. The same label is used for every request made under a particular EST profile. Syntax: arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command?
Syntax: arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command do?

The arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command within the EST profile context, configures the arbitrary re-enrollment label to be concatenated to the EST server URL that is configured with the url command. This label is specific to the re-enrollment operation. There is no arbitrary re-enrollment label configured by default. Any existing arbitrary re-enrollment label is replaced by this command. The use of arbitrary re-enrollment labels is optional. When the re-enrollment label is not configured, the generic arbitrary label (created with the arbitrary- label command) is used (if configured) for re-enrollment. RFC 7030 allows the use of arbitrary labels so that one EST server may serve multiple CAs with the same server URL that gets concatenated with different arbitrary labels. Syntax: arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command?
Syntax: arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki est-profile command do?

The crypto pki est-profile command creates a certificate Enrollment over Secure Transport (EST) profile and changes to the config-est-<EST- NAME> context for the profile. Each EST profile stores information about the EST service, including EST server URL Up to 16 profiles can be created. If the specified EST profile exists, this command changes to the config-est-<EST-NAME> context for the profile. The no form of this command deletes the specified EST profile. It also deletes the TA profiles whose CA certificates were downloaded from the corresponding EST server, and the leaf certificates that were enrolled using this EST profile. NOTE: The deletion of the related TA profiles and enrolled certificates is permanent. Syntax: crypto pki est-profile <EST-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki est-profile command?
Syntax: crypto pki est-profile <EST-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command do?

The reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command within the EST profile context, sets the certificate re-enrollment lead time which is the number of days before certificate expiry date that certificate re-enrollment will be initiated. The no form of this command resets the EST server re-enrollment lead time to its default of 2 days. Syntax: reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command?
Syntax: reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the retry-count <RETRIES> command do?

The retry-count <RETRIES> command within the EST profile context, sets the maximum number of retires to be attempted after the initial certificate enrollment request fails. The no form of this command resets the maximum number of certificate enrollment request retries to its default of 3. Syntax: retry-count <RETRIES>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the retry-count <RETRIES> command?
Syntax: retry-count <RETRIES>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki est-profile command do?
The show crypto pki est-profile command shows a list of all configured EST profiles, or detailed information for a specific profile. Syntax: show crypto pki est-profile [<EST-NAME>].
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki est-profile command?
Syntax: show crypto pki est-profile [<EST-NAME>]
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the url <URL> command do?

The url <URL> command within the EST profile context, configures the URL of the certificate enrollment EST server. This is not configured by default. Any existing URL is replaced by this command. The no form of this command removes the EST server URL within the selected EST profile. The removal of the URL does not affect the TA profiles and enrolled certificates from the EST server. Syntax: url <URL>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the url <URL> command?
Syntax: url <URL>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you configure enhanced security?
To configure enhanced security, follow the documented procedure: 1. Set enhanced security mode: 2. Ensure adequate password requirements: 3. Ensure proper login management as follows: .
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the password complexity command do?

The password complexity command enters the password-complexity context (shown in the switch prompt as config-pwd-cplx) for the purpose of enabling and configuring password complexity. Password complexity enhances security by enforcing specific password complexity requirements. Password complexity is disabled by default and must be enabled by execution of the enable command. The no form of this command reverts all settings to their default values and disables password complexity enforcement. NOTE: To ensure that enhanced security is maintained, it is recommended that you do not set any values to less than their defaults. 184. Syntax: password complexity.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the password complexity command?
Syntax: password complexity
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the cli-session command do?

The cli-session command enters the CLIsession context (shown in the switch prompt as config-cli-session) for the purpose of configuring CLI user session management. Session management enhances security by enforcing specific CLI user session requirements. The following information is provided at time of successful login: • When applicable, the number of failed login attempts since the most recent successful login. • The date, time, and location (console or IP address or hostname) of the most recent previous successful login. • The count of successful logins within the past (configurable) time period. Syntax: cli-session.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the cli-session command?
Syntax: cli-session
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access auth-mode command do?

The aaa authentication port-access auth-mode command configures the authentication mode for the port. By default, client mode is enabled. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-mode {client-mode | device-mode}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access auth-mode command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-mode {client-mode | device-mode}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence command do?

The aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence command configures the per port authentication precedence using the space separator. 192. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x].

syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x]

syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-precedence [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access auth-priority command do?

The aaa authentication port-access auth-priority command configures the authentication priority using the space separator to specific interface. Default auth-priority with concurrent onboarding is 802.1x followed by MAC authentication. With authentication precedence, the default auth-priority follows the auth-precedence order. The no form of the command resets the port access authentication priority to the default, is same as the configured auth-precedence order. The authentication priority is useful in deployments where clients such as wireless access points (APs), IT- compliant-laptops or phones, or laptops without pre-loaded supplicant software must download the supplicant software or firmware patches before attempting 802.1X authentication. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-priority [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x].

syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-priority [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access auth-priority command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-priority [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x]

syntax: aaa authentication port-access auth-priority [dot1x mac-auth | mac-auth dot1x]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access client-limit command do?

The aaa authentication port-access client-limit command configures the maximum number of clients that can simultaneously connect to a port. The no form of this command resets the number of clients to the default. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access client-limit <CLIENTS>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access client-limit command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access client-limit <CLIENTS>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access (role) command do?

The aaa authentication port-access (role) command configures the role to assign to the clients depending on the client authentication state. The no form of the command disassociates the roles that you assign to clients based on the authentication state. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access [critical-role|preauth-role|reject-role|auth-role] <ROLE-NAME>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aaa authentication port-access (role) command?
Syntax: aaa authentication port-access [critical-role|preauth-role|reject-role|auth-role] <ROLE-NAME>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the port-access allow-flood-traffic {enable | disable} command do?

The port-access allow-flood-traffic {enable | disable} command enables or disables transmission of flood traffic, such as broadcast, multicast, and unknown unicast messages through a security enabled port on which no client has been authenticated. By default, transmission of flood traffic is disabled. Configures the fallback role to assign to the clients onboarding on a port. This role is applied only when no derived role is applied to the clients. The no form of the command resets the fallback role. Syntax: port-access allow-flood-traffic {enable | disable}.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the port-access allow-flood-traffic {enable | disable} command?
Syntax: port-access allow-flood-traffic {enable | disable}
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the port-access log-off client mac <MAC-ADDRESS> command do?
The port-access log-off client mac <MAC-ADDRESS> command logs off the client connected to a port access-enabled interface. Syntax: port-access log-off client mac <MAC-ADDRESS>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the port-access log-off client mac <MAC-ADDRESS> command?
Syntax: port-access log-off client mac <MAC-ADDRESS>
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the port-access onboarding-method concurrent <enable | disable> command do?

The port-access onboarding-method concurrent <enable | disable> command configures all methods to start concurrently for faster onboarding process. If authentication priority is not configured when enabling concurrent onboarding, the priority will be 802.1x followed by mac-auth and device-profile. Default priority for concurrent onboarding is 802.1x followed by mac-auth and device-profile. When enabling concurrent onboarding on the port, existing clients will be de-authenticated and freshly onboarded concurrently. When concurrent onboarding is enabled, then auth-precedence will be ignored. If concurrent onboarding is configured, the client will stay in pre-auth role till it gets succeeded by one authentication method or gets failed by all the authentication methods. Syntax: port-access onboarding-method concurrent <enable | disable>.
For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the port-access onboarding-method concurrent <enable | disable> command?
Syntax: port-access onboarding-method concurrent <enable | disable>

section: port-access security violation action shutdown auto-recovery [enable | disable]

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the port-access security violation action shutdown auto-recovery [enable | disable] command do?

The port-access security violation action shutdown auto-recovery [enable | disable] command configures auto-recovery of the port when the security violation action is configured as shutdown. This configuration allows the port that is shut down when a security violation occurs to automatically enable after the recovery timer expires. The no form of the command resets auto-recovery to the default, disable. Syntax: port-access security violation action shutdown auto-recovery [enable | disable].

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method command configures the authentication mechanism used to control access to the network. The configured authentication method will be used to authenticate 802.1X clients. The no form of the command resets the authentication mechanism to the default, eap-radius. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator auth-method eap-radius.

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth- command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth- command configures the period during which an authenticated client, which has failed to reauthenticate because the RADIUS server is unreachable, remains authenticated. The no form of the command resets the cached reauthentication period to the default, 30 seconds. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>.

section: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator eapol-timeout

messages.content: For 6100 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator eapol-timeout command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator eapol-timeout command configure the period the switch waits for a response from a client before retransmitting an EAPOL PDU. If the value is 0, the time period is calculated as per RFC 2988. NOTE: As per RFC 2988 2.1: Before Round-Trip Time (RTT) measurement, set Retransmission Timeout (RTO) to 3 seconds for initial retransmission and then double the RTO to provide back off as per section 5.5. Limit the maximum RTO (RTOmax) to 20 seconds as per section 4.3 of RFC 3748. The no form of the command resets the timeout period to the default. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator eapol-timeout <EAPOL-TIMEOUT>.
